coredata.dc:title: Fluorine-rich graphene quantum dots by selective oxidative cutting of hydroxy fluorographene and their application for sensing of Fe (III) ions
coredata.dc:creator: Sreeja, Kalapparambil
Usha, Mani
Rajeena, Uruniyengal
Raveendran, Poovathinthodiyil
Ramakrishnan, Resmi M.
coredata.dc:description: Nucleophilic substitutions of fluorine moieties on fluorographite by hydroxyl groups are carried out by a strategy in which the parallel reductive defluorinations are minimised. Mild sonochemical exfoliation of the resulting product in water provides an aqueous dispersion of hydroxylated fluorographene (HFG) in which C-F bonds are largely semi-ionic in character. Upon subsequent hydrothermal treatment with hydrogen peroxide, additional fluorinations may take place because of adsorbed fluoride ions. Further, oxidative etching results in the formation of blue fluorescent fluorine-rich small sized (1- 2.5 nm) graphene quantum dots (FGQDs) with excellent solubility and high stability. The prepared FGQDs is shown to be a good sensing platform for the detection of Fe3+ions in aqueous solution. A good linear response is obtained for Fe3+ concentrations over a range of 0–90 µM with a limit of detection of 1.93 µM.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Kerala State Council for Science Technology and Environment

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of HFG and (b) FGQDs and the corresponding histogram (inset).
FTIR spectra of HFG and FGQD.
Raman spectra of HFG and FGQDs.
(a&b) survey XPS spectra, (c&d) C1s spectra and (e&f) F1s spectra of HFG and FGQDs respectively.
Schematic representation of oxidative cutting of HFG into FGQDs with H2O2.

(a) PL spectrum of FGQDs with excitation at 365 nm. Inset is the photograph of FGQDs taken under visible and 365 nm UV light. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of FGQDs solution at different excitation wavelengths. (c) PL and PLE spectra of FGQDs showing the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths.

(a) PL response of aqueous FGQDs solution towards various metal ions.(excitation 320 nm, [Mn+]=100 µM).(b) Fluorescence quenching of FGQDs in the presence of different concentration of Fe3+ (0–90 µM) (c) Stern- Volmer plot for the fluorescence quenching of FGQDs by Fe3+ions.
(a) zeta potential measurement (b) fluorescence decay curve of FGQDs and FGQDs + Fe3+.
A comparison of ratio of C-F covalent to C-F semi-ionic.
comparison of GQDs-based fluorescent sensor for Fe3+detection.
Comparison of Fe3+sensing using different analytical methods.

head.ce:title: Fluorine-rich graphene quantum dots by selective oxidative cutting of hydroxy fluorographene and their application for sensing of Fe (III) ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Sree Neelakanta Govt., Sanskrit College, Pattambi, India -679 303
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Chemistry, Sree Neelakanta Govt., Sanskrit College, Pattambi, India -679 303

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Green Chemistry Research Center (GCRC), Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut, Kerala, 673 635, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bGreen Chemistry Research Center (GCRC), Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut, Kerala, 673 635, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nucleophilic substitutions of fluorine moieties on fluorographite by hydroxyl groups are carried out by a strategy in which the parallel reductive defluorinations are minimised. Mild sonochemical exfoliation of the resulting product in water provides an aqueous dispersion of hydroxylated fluorographene (HFG) in which C-F bonds are largely semi-ionic in character. Upon subsequent hydrothermal treatment with hydrogen peroxide, additional fluorinations may take place because of adsorbed fluoride ions. Further, oxidative etching results in the formation of blue fluorescent fluorine-rich small sized (1- 2.5 nm) graphene quantum dots (FGQDs) with excellent solubility and high stability. The prepared FGQDs is shown to be a good sensing platform for the detection of Fe3+ions in aqueous solution. A good linear response is obtained for Fe3+ concentrations over a range of 0–90 µM with a limit of detection of 1.93 µM.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) form a zero-dimensional, biocompatible, and environment friendly class of nanomaterials with potential applications in sensing. Their unique features are excellent water dispersibility, strong photoluminescence and tunable band gap originating from their characteristic quantum confinement and edge effects [1–8]. Because of these amazing properties, GQDs find applications in several fields such as bio imaging [9], drug delivery [10], and sensing [11–14]. They can also play crucial roles in several advanced state of the art devices including novel photo detectors [15,16], LEDs [17,18], solar cells [19] etc. Chemical modification by the introduction of functional groups or heteroatom doping further enhances the intrinsic structural properties of GQDs and their range of applications [20–24]. Recently, doping of F into GQDs have gained more attention as a means to modulate their electronic and optical properties [25–30] essentially caused by the large electronegativity difference between carbon and fluorine as well as the possibility of covalent, ionic and semi-ionic interaction between these two atoms. Even though there are a large number of reports coming up on GQDs in the recent past, the facile synthetic strategies for fluorinated GQDs, a better understanding of the mechanism of their formation from the precursor graphene sheets by the oxidative cutting process and so on demand further investigations in this direction. Feng et al. [25] have reported the synthesis of fluorinated graphene quantum dots (FG) on hydrothermal treatment resulted in fluorinated graphene quantum dots with an increase in fluorine content than FG. According to the authors significant changes in PL properties are observed on cutting FG to FGQDs. In a work, Gong et al. [27] reported the synthesis of luminescent FGQD with tunable fluorine coverage and size from fluorinated graphite. In their another work, the authors [29] have adopted a fluorine sacrificing strategy to synthesize FGQD from bulk fluorographite.

Fluorographene, initially thought as a 2D analogue of Teflon, turned out as a material with high chemical reactivity owing to the strained geometry of F adatoms in the graphene network [31]. It is reported that the highly labile F atoms in FG are susceptible to reductive defluorination [32] and nucleophilic substitution reactions [33]. Thus, fluorographene can be considered as a promising precursor material for the preparation of versatile graphene derivatives such as hydroxy graphene, cyanographene, graphene acid, alkylated, arylated and alkynylated graphene [34–38]. Very Recently, Huang et al. [39] have reported the covalent modification of FG based on the Suzuki-Miyauara reaction of C-F bond.

A simple and high-yielding method for the preparation of hydroxy graphene from fluorographite was previously reported by Rajeena et al. [40]. In this study, with ethanolic sodium hydroxide reagent, fluorographite (FG) was subjected to nucleophilic substitution reactions by hydroxyl groups. Parallel reductive defluorination were also observed and subsequent exfoliation resulted in the formation of hydroxy graphene (HG), with essentially no fluorine content. In the present study, a subtle modification of this preparation strategy is introduced for the preparation of FGQD precursor graphene sheet in which fluorine atoms are partially retained because substitutions reactions dominate over the reductive defluorinations. The subsequent exfoliation will result in a more hydroxylated fluorographene (HFG) nanosheets, suited for the preparation of FGQDs. For the fine tuning of the fluorine content as well as other parameters which are critical for their evolution as a functional material, it is essential to have a better understanding of the process of defluorination and oxidative etching used in FGQD preparation. Present study can be considered as an attempt in this direction.

Even though metal cations play significant role in biological, environmental and chemical systems, if their concentration in water bodies exceeds the threshold level will pose serious adverse effect on life and hence to be monitored. Instrumental techniques generally used for metal ion detection include atomic absorption spectrometry [40,41], spectrophotometry [42–44], voltammetry [45–48], and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [49,50] which are highly sensitive and selective but requires tedious sample preparation and are also expensive. So recent studies are focussed on fluorescent sensing which is an easy, economical and effective way for detecting metal ions. Fluorine rich graphene quantum dots, we prepared can act as a fluorescent probe for the sensitive detection of Fe3+ions in aqueous solution. Ferric ions are amongst the most essential metal ions in biological systems and its deficiency as well as excess accumulation lead to several diseases including anaemia and even cancer [51,52]. There is a high risk of leaching of iron into water bodies as it is the common constructional material in drinking water pipes, automobiles, buildings, bridges, and also as coagulants in water treatment. Hence the constant monitoring of Fe3+ ions in water bodies is of vital importance. There are several recent reports on the Fe3+ detection using GQDs [11,53], N-GQDs [54–56], S-GQDs [57], B-GQDs [58], N,S-GQDs [59] . However, the studies on the use of fluorinated GQDs as a turn-off fluorescent probe for the selective detection of ferric ions is yet to be explored more.

Fluorinated graphite (>61wt% fluorine) and ferric chloride hexahydrate are purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Sodium hydroxide is purchased from Merck and ethanol from Changshu Hongsheng Fine Chemicals. Ferrous sulphate, manganese chloride, cobalt nitrate, calcium chloride, and nickel nitrate were supplied by Loba Chemie Pvt Ltd., India. Aluminium nitrate, cadmium sulphate, zinc acetate, potassium chloride were purchased from Nice chemicals, India. Lead nitrate is purchased from Sd Fine Chemicals. Mercuric chloride is bought from Fischer Scientific and magnesium sulphate and sodium chloride are from Merck. Silver nitrate is bought from SRL Pvt Ltd. All these commercial reagents are used without further purification. Double distilled water is obtained from EVOQUA (W3T324491) Water Technologies, India.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis is done in a JEOL JEM-2100 microscope operated at 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectra are recorded using (Kratos Analytical) monochromated Al-Kα (1486.6 eV) radiations (15 kV; 250 W, λ=1.5418). FTIR measurements are carried out in a Perkin Elmer Spectrum two L1600300 Fourier Transformation Infra-Red (FTIR) spectrophotometer in the range 4000 cm−1 to 400 cm−1. UV–Vis absorption spectra are recorded on a Perkin Elmer UV WinLab Lamba 850 spectrophotometer. Fluorescence emission spectra are recorded using FL 6500 Perkin Elmer fluorescence spectrofluorimeter. Quantum yield measurements are carried on JY Fluorolog-3–11-spectrofluorimeter. Raman spectra are recorded using WITec alpha control 300RA with 532 nm Argon laser.

To 100 mg of fluorographite, 10 ml of sodium hydroxide solution (10 g NaOH dissolved in 90:10 ethanol-water mixture) is added and the reaction mixture is refluxed at 60 °C for 24 h. The resulting black-coloured dispersion is centrifuged and washed with distilled water and ethanol. The material is finally dried in an oven at 60 °C and then re-dispersed in distilled water by ultrasonication for 3 h resulting in hydroxy fluorographene (HFG) dispersion which is hydrothermally treated with 30% hydrogen peroxide in a Teflon-lined autoclave kept at 180 °C for 1 h.The obtained Fluorine rich graphene quantum dots (FGQDs), is then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min and is stored at 4 °C for further use.

Experimentation was also carried out to study the effect of adsorbed F −ions and Na+ ions. First, the HFG sample prepared using the above-mentioned method was dialysed (MW cut off 3.5 KD) immersing the sample filled dialysis bag in water with continuous stirring for removing the adsorbed fluoride ions and Na+ ions. After the washing, the sample was collected and quantum dots were prepared following the same hydrothermal treatment used earlier.

In a typical assay, 200 µL of FGQD dispersion is added to 2 mL of distilled water taken in a cuvette and the fluorescence spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 335 nm at room temperature. The selectivity of FGQDs is confirmed by addition of standard solutions of metal ions of definite concentration into the sensor system. After 2 min of incubation PL spectrum is recorded. For the sensitivity studies, ferric ions of concentration ranging from 0 to 90 µM are subsequently added to the system and the change in fluorescence intensity were consequently measured at 335 nm excitation wavelength.

Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images reveal the morphology of as synthesised HFG and FGQDs. The TEM image of the HFG (Fig. 1 a) show a good degree of exfoliation since the nucleophilic substituted hydroxyl groups on HFG are sufficiently hydrophilic and hence on sonication water molecules can easily enter into the layers resulting in spontaneous exfoliation. The TEM image of formed FGQDs and the corresponding histogram are depicted in Fig. 1b.It is clearly evidenced from the corresponding histogram that FGQDs formed are of 1–2.5 nm dimensions

FTIR spectroscopy is used to characterize the chemical functional groups in HFG and FGQDs (Fig. 2 ). The peaks at 1104 cm−1 (semi-ionic C-F),1212 cm−1(covalent C-F), 1589cm−1(C = C), 1640 cm−1 (C = O) respectively are found in the spectrum of HFG[60–62]. The strong intense peaks at 1384 cm−1 and 3458 cm−1 confirm the introduction of hydroxyl groups into the fluorographene framework. Nucleophilic substitution with less reductive defluorination on the scaffold of HFG is thus well confirmed from the FTIR spectrum. In FGQDs, the relative intensities of the peak at 1146 cm−1 (semi-ionic C-F) and 1212 cm−1(covalent C-F) are undergoing a substantial change from that of HFG indicative of more covalent C-F bonds than semi-ionic C-F in FGQDs compared to HFG. This points towards further fluorinations taking place in the presence of fluoride moieties during the hydrothermal treatment at 180°C, which results in fluorine rich GQDs with more covalent C-F character[61-66]. Also, the intensity of the peak at 1653 cm−1 (C = O) is increased in FGQDs due to the introduction of carbonyl moieties at the edges during the oxidative etching process.

Atomic force microscopy (Figure S1, supporting information) is used to analyse the degree of exfoliation to HFG sheets. As shown in Figure S1b., the thickness of HFG sheets are of about 4 nm, as revealed from the height profile, which corresponds to few layered sheets [62].

The Raman spectra of HFG and FGQDs are shown in Fig. 3 . Both HFG and FGQDs samples exhibit their disordered band or d- band (associated with the structural defects) at 1357 cm−1, while the G-bands or ordered band (which arise from the in-plane stretching vibration of sp2 domains) of HFG is at 1577 cm−1, and in FGQDs it is red-shifted to 1611 cm−1.The 2D bands of HFG and FGQDs at ∼ 2710 cm−1 are strongly suppressed. The relative D band to G band intensity ratio, ID/IG is 0.15 for HFG and 0.99 for FGQDs. The increase in ID/IG ratio of FGQDs may be attributed due to the presence of more fluorine content and increased edge defects due to the hydrothermal treatment [61].

The elemental composition and chemical bonds of prepared samples were further investigated using XPS analysis. XPS survey spectra of the HFG and FGQDs (Fig. 4 a & b) reveals the presence of elements-carbon, oxygen sodium and fluorine. The sodium peaks observed in both the survey spectra may be from the Na+ ions of NaF or NaOH present in the samples or from the adsorbed Na+ attached to anionic groups present on the graphene surface. Excluding this Na+, the elemental composition in HFG is found to be carbon 62.32%, oxygen 30.79%, and fluorine 6.88% while the C, O and F content in hydroxy graphene sheets prepared using alcoholic NaOH, reported by Rajeena et.al was 80.18%, 18.9%, 0.92% respectively. This result suggests that addition of water in alcoholic sodium hydroxide reagent have triggered more substitution reactions in HFG with a lesser extent of reductive defluorinations. Similarly, the C, O and F contents in FGQDs are found to be 65.27%, 16.67%, 18.06% respectively. The decrease in oxygen content as well as increase in fluorine content observed in FGQDs from that of HFG during the hydrothermal treatment throw light into the mechanism of oxidative cutting. In previous studies, there are reports about the chemical modifications of oxidised graphene nanosheets during the hydrothermal treatment by fluoride ions. These fluorinations take place by replacing the oxygen containing groups and also the degree of fluorination as well as the nature of C-F bond configurations can be controlled by the reaction temperature, time and amount of F − ions [61–66]. In the present work as well, hydrothermal treatment of HFG with adsorbed fluoride ions (as evident from F1s XPS spectra), some degree of fluorinations take place replacing oxygen containing groups are evident and thereby an increase in covalent C-F bonds in the formed FGQDs, as supported from the FTIR and XPS analysis .

A detailed investigation of the chemical composition of HFG and FGQDs by high resolution C 1 s XPS spectra are shown in Fig. 4. The high resolution C 1 s spectra of HFG and FGQDs can be deconvoluted into 6 components. In HFG, the peaks are at 284.6 eV(C=C), 285.4 eV(C-C), 286.4(C-O), 287.4(C=O), and 288 eV (C-F semi-ionic), 289 eV (covalent C-F). In the corresponding spectrum of FGQDs, peaks are at 284.6 eV(C=C), 285.6 eV(C-C), 286.5 eV(C-O), 287.1 eV(C=O), 288.8 eV(C-F semi-ionic), 290.6 eV (covalent C-F) [67–69]. The F1s high resolution spectra of HFG provide 3 peaks corresponding to adsorbed F−, C-F semi-ionic, and C-F covalent at 684.2 eV, 687.4 eV, 688.4 eV respectively, while in the spectrum of FGQDs, the corresponding values are at 683.7 eV, 687.8 eV and 688.5 eV respectively[70,71]. The decrease in adsorbed fluorine content (Fig. 4f) as well as oxygen containing functional groups as obtained in XPS spectra of FGQDs are consistent with our suggested mechanism. A comparison of C-F covalent to C-F semi-ionic ratio for FG, HFG and FGQDs are summarised in Table 1 and it is clear that a drastic reduction of covalent C-F bond population is seen in the conversion of fluorographite (FG) to hydroxy fluorographene (HFG) which emphasize the nucleophilic substitution with less reductive fluorination in FG on treatment with aqueous alcoholic NaOH. However, when HFG is converted to FGQDs, the ratio is found to be slightly increasing, indicating more covalent C-F bond population in FQDs which indicates fluorination taking place in HFG on hydrothermal treatment in presence of adsorbed fluoride ions.

For getting a better understanding of the effect of residual Na+ and F − ions, HFG was subjected to dialysis prior to hydrothermal treatment and the as-obtained HFG and quantum dots resulted from the hydrothermal treatment was analysed using XPS and the results are provided in the supplementary data (Fig. 3S & Table 1S). In the high-resolution F1s spectrum of the dialysed HFG, the adsorbed fluorine peak vanished completely indicating there are no adsorbed fluoride ions left in the sample whereas there were some residual Na+ ions even after washing, probably adsorbed to the anionic groups of graphene. After the removal of NaF, the high-resolution XPS for C and F were analysed and found that the relative compositions have not changed substantially, especially the covalent C-F to semi-ionic C-F showed only a small change. The quantum dots prepared from washed HFG contained lesser fluorine content than the precursor HFG, probably because of the lack of adsorbed fluorine required for further fluorination process during the fluorine rich quantum dot formation.
A schematic representation of the quantum dot formation from HFG is shown in Fig. 5 .

FGQDs emit blue fluorescence when irradiated with 365 nm UV light, as shown in Fig. 6 (a). To further explore the optical properties of FGQDs, photoluminescence spectral measurements are carried out. It is observed that the emission wavelength of FGQDs depends on the excitation wavelengths used. As the excitation wavelength is changed from 295 to 365 nm, the PL peak also is getting shifted to longer wavelengths (Fig. 6b). The excitation-dependence of PL of FGQDs is thought to be resulting from the non-uniformity in sizes of the quantum dots and also due to the presence of emissive traps present in FGQDs [28]. FGQDs exhibit strong emission peak at about 450 nm when excited with 335 nm. The obtained PL and PLE spectra of FGQDs are presented in Fig. 6c. Using quinine sulphate as the standard, the PL quantum yield of FGQDs is estimated which is found to be 2.9% (ESI).

To explore the possibility of FGQDs as a fluorescent sensor towards detection of metallic ions, the fluorescence intensity measurement of FGQDs in the presence of various metal ions are carried out. The relative fluorescence intensities are measured after the addition of 20 µL of 0.1 mM solutions of metal ions (Cd2+, Hg2+, Ni2+,Mn2+,Mg2+,Cu2+,Pb2+,Fe2+,Fe3+,Al3+,Ca2+,Zn2+,Na+,K+,Co2+,Ag+) into the FGQDs and the results are presented in Fig. 7 (a). Out of the 16 metal ions tested, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) of FGQDs have lowered on addition of Cu2+, Pb2+, Fe2+and only in the case of Fe3+ ions, an almost complete fluorescence quenching is observed. Since Cu2+, Pb2+, and Fe2+ions have quenching effect on the fluorescence of FGQDs, FGQDs can only act as a fluorescent probe under limited circumstances, when it is known that Fe3+ is the only quencher present. Based on the related literature reports [72–74], ferric ions may be getting engaged in non-specific interactions with the surface functional hydroxy /carboxy groups of FGQDs which leads to FL quenching. The sensitivity of FGQDs with different Fe3+ion concentrations is analysed and it is found that the FL intensity of FGQDs is decreasing linearly with increased concentration of ferric ions. Figure 7(b) depicts the fluorescence quenching effect on FGQDs with Fe3+ ion concentrations varying from 0 to 90 µM. From the Stern-Volmer plot, shown in Fig. 7(c), the FL intensity ratio (F/F0) show a good linear response with Fe3+ over the concentration range 0 to 90 µM with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9901. The limit of detection (LOD) of Fe3+ was calculated to be 1.93 µM based on 3σ/m where σ is the standard deviation of blank signal and m is the slope of the linear calibration plot. A comparison of other GQDs-Fe3+ sensor system are provided in Table 2 and Fe3+ ion sensing using different analytical methods are discussed in Table 3 .

To understand the mechanism for fluorescence quenching, we have calculated the quenching constant (Ksv) from the slope of linear fit of Stern –Volmer plot given by equation F0/F=1+Ksv[Q] where F0 and F are FL intensities of FGQDs in the presence and absence of Fe3+ ion at an excitation wavelength of 335 nm respectively and [Q] is the concentration of quencher. The quenching constant (Ksv) which denotes the binding affinity between the fluorescence molecule and quenching molecule is found to be 4.34 *103 M−1. The good linearity of Stern-Volmer plot over the measured concentration range and also the higher value of Ksv indicate a static quenching between FGQDs and Fe3+ [75,76].

Zeta potential measurements are used to find the surface charge of nanoparticles which governs its stability. Studies report that nanoparticles with zeta potential in the range of −30 to 30 mV exhibits high colloidal stability [77,78]. Surface charge measured for FGQDs is −36.8 mV due to surface rich hydroxyl groups which imparts its high stability (Fig. 8 a). After the addition of Fe3+, the zeta potential measurement is decreased to −30.8 mV which suggest the quenching mechanism involves complexation of Fe3+ions with the surface hydroxy/carboxy groups. Life time measurement obtained by measuring the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of quencher molecule is a useful technique to distinguish static quenching from dynamic quenching [79,80]. Under optimal experimental condition, before and after the addition of Fe3+, the fluorescence life time for FGQDs remains almost constant (7.8 ns) indicating a static quenching mechanism (Fig. 8b). Based on above results, we suggest that the addition of Fe3+into the FGQD solution can result in the formation of a stable complex in its ground state which facilitates a non-radiative electron-hole recombination annihilation and thus triggering the quenching of fluorescence.

In this study, a novel method is adopted for the synthesis of hydroxylated fluorographene (HFG) from fluorographite, through nucleophilic substitution of labile fluorine atoms by hydroxyl groups, minimizing the extent of reductive defluorination in the graphene network. The hydrothermal treatment of prepared HFG dispersion with adsorbed fluoride ion resulted in additional fluorinations and oxidative etching producing fluorine-rich graphene quantum dots (FGQDs). The obtained FGQDs exhibit blue fluorescence and good water solubility. The feasibility of the prepared FGQDs to function as a label-free fluorescent probe for the selective detection of Fe 3+ions through a static quenching mechanism is further explored here. The sensitive response of FGQDS with Fe3+concentration is found to follow a linear relationship over the range of 0–90 µM with a detection limit of 1. 93 µM.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jfluchem.2023.110130.

Science
